topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammering started precisely at seven in the morning, a rhythmic, insistent clang that seemed to vibrate through the thin plaster of my bedroom walls. I kept my eyes fixed on the ceiling, tracing the hairline cracks with my gaze, willing the noise to cease. Every few minutes, I’d tense, my jaw tightening until a dull ache bloomed behind my molars. When I finally forced myself to look out the window, I saw him—Mr. Henderson, my neighbor, meticulously measuring lengths of wood with an almost obsessive focus. His movements were too precise, too deliberate for a simple fence installation. A cold prickle ran down my spine, making the fine hairs on my arms stand rigid. I retreated under the heavy duvet, pulling the covers up to my chin, trying to make myself small and invisible against the intrusion.

[story 2]

I kept glancing through the blinds, half-expecting to see him in the yard, but the morning remained stubbornly silent. That silence, however, was worse. It felt heavy, like a blanket smothering the light. I found myself pacing the length of my living room floor, my feet shuffling against the hardwood, a restless energy churning in my gut. Each time I stopped, I would press my palms against my eyes, as if trying to physically block out what I suspected was coming. The thought of that new barrier going up, dividing our properties, made my stomach clench with a strange, tight knot. I kept replaying the conversation from last week—his overly cheerful insistence about needing 'more privacy'—and a faint tremor started in my hands.

[story 3]

It was the smell that truly caught me first: sawdust mixed with something sharp, almost metallic. I stood by the kitchen window, watching the edge of the lawn where Mr. Henderson was staking the first post. He hummed a tuneless melody while he worked, a sound that grated against my nerves like sandpaper on wood. I tried to focus on the mundane—the way the sunlight caught the dust motes dancing in the air—but my attention kept snapping back to him. He didn't look up, his concentration absolute, and that refusal to acknowledge my presence felt like a deliberate dismissal. A slow, creeping sense of alarm began to coil in my chest, making my breath catch in my throat. I needed a distraction, anything to break the suffocating tension building inside me.
